title: The Legend of the Indian Paintbrush
author: Traditional

content.text: The Legend of the Indian Paintbrush  Long ago, when the world was still young and the Great Plains stretched wide beneath the endless sky, there lived a boy named Little Gopher.
He was not like the other boys in his tribe.
While they ran swift as deer and shot arrows straight as the wind, Little Gopher was small and quiet.
His legs were not strong, and his hands trembled when he tried to hold a bow.
But though he could not hunt or race, he had a gift—one that filled his heart with wonder.
Little Gopher loved to draw.
From the moment the sun rose, painting the prairie gold, until it dipped below the horizon in a blaze of red and purple, he would sit with a stick and scratch pictures into the earth.
He drew the buffalo herds thundering across the land, the eagles soaring high above, and the faces of his people, smiling as they danced around the fire.

The elders would watch him and say, "This boy sees the world with different eyes."  One evening, as the stars began to twinkle like fireflies in the darkening sky, Little Gopher sat by himself, tracing shapes in the dirt.
A voice, soft as the whisper of the wind, spoke to him.
"Little Gopher," it said, "why do you draw in the earth when your pictures could live forever?"  Little Gopher looked up, but no one was there.
The voice came again. "Go to the place where the sun sets.

There, you will find what you need to make your visions real."  The next morning, Little Gopher packed a small bundle of food and set off toward the western hills, where the sun dipped below the earth each night.
He walked for many days, through valleys where wildflowers nodded in the breeze and over ridges where the wind sang strange, sweet songs.
At last, he came to a high hill covered in rocks of every color—red as the sunset, blue as the deepest lake, yellow as the morning sun, and white as the clouds.
As he climbed, the voice returned.
"Take these," it said, "and use them to paint the stories of your people."  Little Gopher knelt and gathered the smooth, colorful stones.
He crushed them between two rocks, mixing the powders with water from a nearby stream.
Then, with a brush made from the fur of a rabbit, he began to paint.
He painted the sacred dances of his tribe, the warriors riding into battle, and the women singing as they gathered berries.
He painted the animals—the swift antelope, the wise old owl, and the mighty bear.
And as he painted, something magical happened.
The colors glowed as if lit from within, brighter than any he had ever seen.
When he finished, he stepped back and gasped.
The pictures were not just on the rocks—they shimmered in the air, as if the spirits themselves had breathed life into them.
The voice spoke one last time. "Little Gopher, you have been given a great gift.
From this day forward, you will be known as He-Who-Brought-the-Colors-to-the-Earth.
And the brush you hold will be called the Indian Paintbrush, for it will bring beauty to all who see it."  Little Gopher returned to his village, his heart full of joy.
The people gathered around as he showed them his paintings, and their faces lit up with wonder.
From that day on, whenever someone saw a field of wildflowers with petals as bright as the colors Little Gopher had used, they would say, "Look!
The Indian Paintbrush has visited us again!"  And so, the legend lived on.
Even now, if you walk through the prairies in the springtime, you might see the little red flowers nodding in the wind—the same flowers that Little Gopher used to paint the stories of his people.
And if you listen very closely, you might hear the wind whispering his name. The End.
